passage: The old Hargrove house had been empty for eleven years and it showed in the way empty houses always showed — a particular quality of stillness that felt less like absence and more like waiting. Holly pushed the front door open with her shoulder and it gave with a groan that seemed disproportionate to the effort. She swept her flashlight across the entryway: peeling wallpaper, a chandelier missing half its pendants, floorboards that bowed slightly at the center. She was not afraid of old houses. She had grown up in old houses. She told herself this and moved further inside, her footsteps loud in the silence. The beam of the flashlight caught something in the far corner — just a coat rack, she saw, a coat rack with something hanging from it — and her heart made its unasked-for comment. She steadied the beam and identified the object as a rain slicker, old and stiff, and exhaled slowly. Outside, the wind moved through the eaves in a way that produced a sound she did not care for. She checked the window latches and found them all unsecured and locked the two she could reach. She made a note to come back in daylight.

The phone call had been three minutes of careful, controlled language from a man who did not do careful and controlled, and she had spent the twenty minutes since it ended sitting at her kitchen table trying to determine what the careful and controlled was covering. She had known Marcus Hale for six years and she knew the register he used when something was wrong, and that had been the register. She turned her coffee mug in her hands and went through the conversation again, listening for the thing beneath the words. He had asked twice if she was home. He had asked if she had plans to go out. He had said to stay inside if she could manage it, phrasing it as a casual suggestion in a tone that was not casual. She looked at her front door and verified that she had locked it when she came in, which she had. She got up and checked the back door as well, and the window above the kitchen sink, and then stood in her kitchen and waited for the phone to ring again.

The man had been at the far end of the bar for an hour, nursing the same drink, and looking at her with the particular quality of attention she had learned to categorize and file. She kept her eyes on the conversation she was having and tracked him in her peripheral vision the way she had taught herself to do. He was not doing anything. He was sitting and drinking and looking, which were all things people in bars did, and none of which obligated her to feel the way she felt. She finished her drink and said her goodbyes and found her coat and calculated the distance to the door and the distance from the door to her car and the route between them. The exit took her past him. He said nothing as she passed and she did not look at him and she was through the door and in the parking lot with the cold air on her face. She found her keys before she reached her car, the way she always did, the way she had done since she was nineteen years old, and she was in the car and the doors were locked before she breathed normally again.

The letter had no return address and the handwriting was not anyone she recognized. She set it on the table and made herself finish her coffee before she opened it, because whatever it contained was going to be the same thing whether she read it now or in five minutes, and five minutes of not-knowing seemed preferable to five minutes of knowing. She opened it. Inside was a newspaper clipping — local paper, two years old — about a house fire in which no one had been hurt. She had lived in that house. She turned the clipping over and found nothing written on the back and turned it over again and read it a second time as though it would yield more on second reading, which it did not. She put it back in the envelope and put the envelope in the drawer beside the phone. She sat for a while at the kitchen table. She got up and checked that the deadbolt was engaged and then went to the window and looked out at the street, which was empty and ordinary and gave her nothing.

She had been hearing the same car for three days — not the same route, which would have been explainable, but the same car appearing at different times and different locations in a pattern that had too much variation to be coincidence and not enough to be something else. A dark blue sedan, American make, mid-nineties, with a dent in the rear quarter panel. She had noted the license plate on the second day. She had not yet decided what to do with the note. She told herself there were a hundred explanations for a car appearing in the same radius as a person three days running in a small town, and she believed all of them individually and none of them collectively. She changed her route to work and varied the timing by forty minutes in either direction and drove past her building twice before parking, which were things she had not done in years and had hoped not to do again. The car did not appear on day four and she told herself she had been imagining it and mostly believed this.

The trail through the state forest was well-marked and she had run it a hundred times and she knew every turn and root and muddy section of it, which was why it registered when something was different. The blaze on the oak at the half-mile marker had been repainted — brighter than the others, recent. She slowed without stopping and looked at it and kept moving. Someone had been on this trail recently with paint and a reason for painting. She catalogued this and kept running but ran with the particular quality of attention she used in situations she had not fully assessed. The light was good and the trail was familiar and she was fine. She told herself she was fine. She noted the second repainted blaze at the three-quarter mile marker and turned around at the one-mile point instead of completing the loop, which added forty minutes to her return but was the choice she made.

She found the back door unlocked when she came home, which could have meant she had forgotten to lock it and probably meant she had forgotten to lock it and was the kind of thing she would normally dismiss and could not dismiss tonight, after the week she had been having. She stood on the back porch with her key in her hand and looked at the door and the yard and the garage and the windows of the house, which were all dark and unrevealing. She had left at seven-fifteen this morning. The door looked undisturbed — no marks on the frame, no damage to the lock. She pushed it open and stood in the doorway and listened to the house before she went into it. The house was quiet in the way houses were quiet when they were empty, which she had learned to distinguish from the quiet of a house that was not. She went through it room by room anyway, checking each one before she moved on, and found it empty and ordinary and exactly as she had left it, and was not entirely reassured.

The thing about working late in an office building was that the building itself changed register after eight o'clock — the HVAC running more prominently, the distant sounds of the city taking on a different character, the elevator opening and closing on other floors for reasons she could not account for. She had worked late in this building for four years and none of this had ever bothered her and it was bothering her tonight, which she attributed to the email. The email had come at six-forty-seven, from an address she did not recognize, containing only her full name and her office number and the words: I know you're still there. She had forwarded it to IT and to her manager and had debated going home and had decided not to be driven out of her own office by an email that was probably nothing. The elevator opened on her floor and she listened to the footsteps that followed and tracked them to the office three doors down from hers, which was where David worked and where David was apparently still working. She let out a breath and returned to her screen.

He had left the party before she did and she had noticed and then told herself she had not noticed, and then the night air outside the party was cold and the parking lot was badly lit and she noticed again. She found her car in the second row where she had left it and checked the backseat before she got in, which was something she had read about and implemented as habit rather than necessity. The back seat was empty. She started the car and pulled out of the lot and the road back to town was empty at this hour, which was normal for a Tuesday in October. A vehicle turned onto the road behind her — its headlights appearing at the junction, keeping pace. She adjusted her speed slightly upward and the headlights kept pace. She turned on a road she did not normally use and drove two minutes and turned again and the headlights were not behind her and she drove the rest of the way home with the radio on and parked in the lighted space near the entrance of her building.

The old mine road had not been maintained since the late seventies and driving it at dusk was the kind of decision she would not have made an hour earlier but was committed to now, with four miles still between her and the highway. The headlights picked up ruts and loose rock and the occasional suggestion of the road's edge, beyond which the hillside fell away to a dry creek bed she could hear but not see. She drove slowly and the truck obliged her with its four-wheel drive and she did not look at the drop on the passenger side more than twice. At the two-mile mark, another vehicle's lights appeared in her rearview mirror — back where the junction was, which meant someone had followed her onto the road or was also driving the road for their own reasons. She kept her speed and watched the lights in the mirror and they fell back when she went around a curve and did not reappear. She reached the highway with her hands aching from the grip she had maintained on the wheel and turned north toward town.

She had been in the house alone for three nights while her sister was in Portland, which was not a thing that normally required noting, and she was noting it because of the marks on the back gate. Two parallel scrape marks in the wood, fresh enough to still be pale against the weathered gray of the fence — consistent with someone lifting the latch and pushing through. She had taken photographs and looked at them on her phone and decided they were consistent with a neighbor's dog or a deer or any number of things that were not concerning. She locked the back gate from the inside with the secondary latch and she put the kitchen light on a timer and she called her neighbor on the left and asked, casually, if she had noticed anything odd and the neighbor had not noticed anything odd. She made dinner and ate it and watched television and went to bed and lay awake for forty minutes listening to the ordinary sounds of the house and then fell asleep.

The man at the farmers market had been helpful — too helpful, following her from stall to stall, offering assistance with her bags, asking questions about where she lived in the guise of conversation. She had deflected each question without answering it, which she had done without making it obvious, which was a skill she had developed and was tired of needing. She thanked him at the end of the market and walked to her car by a route that went through the center of the crowd rather than the edge. He did not follow her into the crowd. She checked her mirrors backing out of the parking spot and he was standing at the edge of the market watching her pull away, his hands in his pockets, which was not threatening behavior and was not nothing. She drove to her grocery store, which was in the opposite direction from her house, and spent forty minutes buying things she did not urgently need.

The knocking had started at two in the morning — four knocks, then silence, then four more, then silence — and it was coming from the front door, which was the door no one she knew used. Her friends came to the back. She lay in bed and listened to the silence between the knocks and decided. She did not go to the door. She picked up her phone and looked at it and the knocking came again and she turned on the lamp on the nightstand and sat up and listened. The house was a hundred-year-old farmhouse and made its own sounds at night and she had catalogued those sounds over three years of living in it and the knocking was not one of them. She waited fifteen minutes in the light and the knocking did not come again and she turned the lamp off and lay in the dark and did not sleep for another hour.

The woman who had been watching her in the coffee shop was still there when she got back from the bathroom, still watching, still with the same half-cup of coffee that had been the same half-cup when she had noticed her forty minutes ago. She gathered her things with a deliberateness that she hoped read as simply being finished rather than being aware, and she left the coffee shop and walked half a block and looked in a shop window that gave her a view of the street behind her. The woman came out of the coffee shop. The woman stood on the sidewalk for a moment and looked in the direction she had walked and then walked the other direction. She watched until the woman had rounded the corner and then stood for another minute in case the woman came back around the block. She took a different route to her next appointment than she had planned.

She had been receiving the calls for two weeks — always late in the evening, always the same number, always silence when she answered, and then a disconnection at the thirty-second mark. She had reported them to her carrier and the carrier had told her what carriers tell you and she had downloaded a call-blocking app and blocked the number and received the next call from a different number, different area code, same silence, same thirty seconds. She had told no one because telling someone would require her to explain why she was worried and explaining why she was worried would require her to explain things she had not explained in four years. She added a deadbolt to her front door, which she had been meaning to do. She bought a better outdoor motion-sensor light for the back of the house, which she had also been meaning to do. She slept with her phone on the nightstand, which she had always done, but now it was plugged in at a length that reached from the nightstand to her hand if she reached for it in the dark.

The hunting cabin where they had agreed to meet was dark when she arrived, which could mean he was late and could mean something else. She sat in her car with the engine running for three minutes and looked at the dark windows and the dark porch and the surrounding tree line, which was uniformly dark and uniformly useless in terms of information. She had a flashlight and she had her phone and she had the knowledge that cell coverage ended at the bottom of the mountain road. She had told her neighbor where she was going, which was the habit she had maintained since she was twenty-three. She got out of the car and crossed to the porch and tried the door, which was unlocked, and looked into the dark interior without going in. She called his name. The cabin gave back silence and the smell of cold wood ash. She went back to her car and waited with the doors locked and the engine on and the headlights pointed at the cabin door.

She found the note under her windshield wiper in the hospital parking structure, which was not an unusual thing to find except that the note was not the usual kind. It was a single index card with her license plate number written on it in block capitals, and below that, in the same handwriting: I see you every day. She looked at it for a long moment and then looked at the parking structure around her, the other cars, the shadows between the pillars, the elevators. The structure was, as far as she could determine, empty. She put the note in her pocket and got in her car and sat for a moment before starting it. She had been parking in this structure for two years. She was going to need to park somewhere else. She told herself this was an overreaction and drove home and parked in the structure anyway and went to her apartment and turned all the lights on and sat with her cup of tea and thought about which colleague she was going to mention this to in the morning.

He was standing on the sidewalk outside her building when she got home, which would have been unremarkable if she had not seen him in the same place the night before. She was almost certain it was the same man. Tall, dark jacket, standing with the particular stillness of someone with no obvious reason to be standing there. She slowed her car to read the situation and kept moving and turned into the underground garage rather than parking on the street as she usually did. She sat in the garage after she parked and waited to see if he had followed the car. He had not come down the ramp. She rode the elevator from the garage to her floor and went to her window and looked down at the sidewalk and the man was gone and the sidewalk was empty and the streetlight was doing its normal job. She made dinner and ate it and called her friend Rachel for company, and did not mention the man.

The sound from the basement was intermittent and had been happening for three nights — a slow, rhythmic sound, like something rocking, something wooden moving against concrete. She had been in the basement during the day and found nothing and had told herself the house was settling, which was what old houses did. At eleven-fifteen on the third night the sound came again and she lay in bed and listened to it with the methodical attention she brought to things she had decided were probably nothing. It was probably nothing. The sound came again, three repetitions, then stopped. She got up and she stood at the top of the basement stairs and turned on the light and looked down the staircase, which showed her the basement floor and the furnace and the shelving along the near wall. She called down into the space, feeling the absurdity of this. The sound did not come again that night.

The gallery was empty at five-thirty on a Wednesday and she had been so absorbed in the work on her desk that she had not heard the other members of the staff leave. She became aware of the emptiness gradually, the way you became aware of a sound that had stopped. She pushed back from her desk and looked through the glass partition at the main gallery space, which was lit and still and full of the particular quality of occupied absence that galleries had after hours. She walked through it to check the front door, which was locked, and then to the back corridor and the service entrance, which was also locked. There was no reason for anyone to be in the building. There was no one in the building. She went back to her desk and worked for another forty minutes and then gathered her things and let herself out through the front and stood on the sidewalk in the October evening and breathed the outdoor air with an appreciation she could not have explained to anyone.

She was three miles from the trailhead when she heard the other footsteps — not behind her, which would have been easier to locate, but somewhere to her left in the trees, moving at approximately her pace, not on the trail. She stopped and listened and the footsteps stopped. She told herself: deer, which was the correct and likely answer. She started walking and after thirty seconds the sound in the trees started again. She changed her pace — faster for twenty steps, then slower — and the sound in the trees changed with her, which deer did not do. She looked at her phone and found two bars of signal and noted this and kept moving. The sound continued for another quarter mile and then stopped and did not resume and she reached the trailhead in the late afternoon light and got in her car and sat for a moment before starting the engine.

The man who had moved into the apartment at the end of her floor had introduced himself as Kevin, had been pleasant, and had asked within the first five minutes of conversation what her work schedule was. She had answered vaguely, which was her practice with new neighbors, and he had asked again, more specifically, and she had answered vaguely again and ended the conversation and gone back to her apartment and stood in her kitchen thinking about the two questions. People asked about schedules for many reasons. She had learned which reasons to pay attention to at some cost and she was not going to unlearn that lesson in the name of being charitable to a man she had known for six minutes. She mentioned the new neighbor to her friend Dana and described the conversation and Dana said she was probably reading into it and she said probably and remained of her original opinion.

She had not realized until she was back in her car that the man at the end of the bar had been listening to her phone call. She had been talking to her sister about the house — the address, the showing time, the fact that she would be there alone for the walkthrough — and he had been three stools away, facing forward but with the particular stillness of someone engaged with something other than their drink. She sat in her car and went through what she had said and assembled the information it had offered to someone paying attention. It was a lot of information. She called her sister back and asked her to meet her at the showing, and when her sister asked why she said she would explain later, and she changed the showing time with the realtor by ninety minutes.

The gravel road to the farmhouse was a mile long and ran between two fields with no tree cover, which meant anyone coming down it was visible from the house well before they arrived, which had always seemed like an advantage and seemed like something else now. She watched the headlights come down the road and there was no reason for headlights on her road at ten-forty on a Thursday and she watched them until they reached the junction with the county road and turned east without slowing. She stayed at the window for another five minutes. The dog was watching something through the glass door to the back porch, his ears forward, his tail doing nothing. She opened the door to the back porch and stood in it and looked at the yard and the outbuildings and the dark fields beyond them, listening. The night gave her nothing specific. She brought the dog inside and locked the back door and the dog settled on the kitchen floor and she went back to what she had been doing.

The thing about the text message was that she had not given this number to anyone except the six people she trusted most, and the text was not from any of them. It read: I heard about the promotion. Congratulations. She looked at it for a long time, turning over the implications. The promotion had been announced internally on Tuesday morning in a meeting she had attended with eleven colleagues. She had not posted about it anywhere. She had told her sister and her two closest friends. She had not told anyone who would have had this number. She screenshot the message and sent it to her IT security contact at work and reported the number as unknown to her and spent the rest of the afternoon with the low-grade discomfort of something she could not categorize and therefore could not dismiss.

She drove past the house twice before she pulled into the driveway, and she could not have said precisely what had made her drive past it the first time except that something about the house's appearance at dusk had registered as different without her being able to identify the difference. On the second pass she placed it: the kitchen light was off. She left the kitchen light on a timer when she traveled, always had, and the timer was set for five-thirty and it was five-fifty. She sat in the driveway with the car running and looked at the house. The front door was closed. The windows were closed. The porch light was on, as it should be. She got out of the car and stood in the driveway and looked at the kitchen window and then walked around the side of the house to look at the back, which was as she had left it. She went in through the front door and stood in the hallway and listened to the house and the kitchen light was on a different timer than she remembered setting and she decided this was the explanation.

She was used to the night sounds of the ranch and she knew them the way she knew her own heartbeat — the barn settling, the horses moving, the coyotes at the far edge of the property — and the sound that woke her at two in the morning was none of them. It was a light sound, almost nothing, like a door moving in a draft. She lay still and listened and it did not come again. She got up and looked at the window: dark yard, no movement, the barn a dark shape at the usual distance. She put on her robe and went to the top of the stairs and looked down into the hall, which was dark and empty and smelled like the house always smelled. She checked the front door, which was locked, and the back door, which was locked, and the window by the kitchen sink, which was closed. She went back to bed and lay in the dark and the sound did not come again and she told herself in the morning she would check the barn.

The Instagram account that had started following her photography page had no posts and no profile photo and followed only three accounts, one of which was hers. She had six thousand followers and followed accounts like this came and went and she had never looked at them twice. She looked at this one twice because it had followed her at two in the morning on the same night she had gotten home late from a shoot and had driven home by an unusual route because of construction and the account had liked the photo she had posted from that route, seventeen seconds after she had posted it. She reported the account to the platform and blocked it and called her friend Marcus, who worked in digital security, and described the sequence of events. He was quiet for a moment and then asked if she was somewhere she felt safe, and the question itself was its own kind of answer.

She had been to the storage unit twice in the past month and both times she had been certain the padlock was in a different position than she had left it — not opened, not forced, but repositioned, slightly, as though someone had tried it and found it locked and set it back at a different angle than they had found it. She inspected the lock each time for marks that would indicate a pick or a bump and found nothing conclusive. She told herself she was imagining the angle. She was not the kind of person who imagined things about padlocks. She went through the inventory of what was in the unit: boxes of her sister's things, her old furniture, the filing cabinet with her financial records, the hard drives she kept there as backup. She moved the filing cabinet to the back wall and stacked boxes in front of it. She changed the padlock to a model with a hidden shackle and drove home.

The shortcut through the parking structure took four minutes off her walk home and she had been taking it for a year without incident and she was not going to stop taking it because of one night, but she was aware of the night in question in a way she had not been aware of the preceding three hundred and sixty-four. The structure was lit at its entrances and darker in the middle, which had always been true, and the sound of her own footsteps on the concrete had always echoed, which was also always true. A car engine started somewhere above her and she tracked the sound to the third floor and watched the ramp for the headlights, which appeared and descended in an ordinary way and passed her on its way out without slowing. She reached the far side of the structure and the street beyond it and stood for a moment in the ordinary light of the ordinary street and felt the ordinary return to her.

She had found the door to her office ajar twice in the same week, on mornings when she was first in and certain she had closed it the night before. Nothing had been taken. Nothing had been moved, as far as she could determine. She mentioned it to the building manager, who had explained that the latch mechanism on that door was worn and known to not catch reliably in damp weather, which was a plausible explanation. She listened to the explanation and thanked him for it and went back to her office and looked at the latch mechanism, which was indeed worn. She looked at her desk, which was as she had left it, and at the filing cabinet, which was locked, and at the window, which looked down into the building's internal courtyard. She had the latch mechanism replaced that afternoon, at her own expense, and felt better about it than the seven dollars it had cost seemed to warrant.

The hiking trail was marked as moderate and she had done it before, but she had not done it alone and in late October with the light failing two hours earlier than in summer. She had her headlamp and her emergency kit and she had told two people where she was going and given them her expected return time. She was noting all of this because the trail had been empty since the first mile and the sound she had been hearing for the past ten minutes was keeping pace with her on the uphill side, just inside the tree line, which was too deliberate for wind and too large for a bird. She had seen deer and elk on this trail and had no reason to discount either, and she kept hiking and kept noting the sound and when it fell silent at the two-mile marker she found she missed it less than she would have expected.

The gun in her desk drawer had been there since she bought the house, five years ago, and she had not looked at it in two years and she looked at it now because of the car outside. The car had been there for four days — not always, but regularly, parked on the opposite side of the street with a sightline to her front door. She had written down the plate number and the make and model and she had not yet called it in because calling it in felt like a declaration that she was frightened, which she was not, precisely. She was alert. She knew the difference. She looked at the gun and closed the drawer and made herself dinner and ate it at the table where she could see the street through the window, and the car was there, and at nine-fifteen it was not there, and she washed the dishes and made the call.

She had met the new ranch manager twice and found nothing alarming about him, which was not the same as finding nothing to note. He was efficient and knowledgeable and asked good questions about the property and answered her questions directly, and he carried a hunting knife on his belt, which every man in this part of Montana did, and she was aware of it in the way she was not usually aware of knives on belts, which told her something about the week she was having. She showed him the equipment barn and the bunkhouse and the fence line on the south pasture and he asked sensible questions and took sensible notes and when he left he shook her hand with the same handshake with which he had arrived. She stood in the driveway and watched his truck go down the road and then called her foreman and asked what he knew about the man, which was more than he had said at the recommendation, which was itself interesting.

The lights in the adjacent apartment came on at three in the morning, which was unusual but not unprecedented — her neighbor worked odd hours. What was unusual was the sound that accompanied the lights, which was not the sound of someone arriving home but the sound of someone moving furniture, carefully, at three in the morning, in a way that suggested the care was about not being heard rather than about not waking the neighbors. She lay in bed and mapped the sounds against the layout of the apartment, which she had been in twice for social reasons and knew reasonably well. The sounds moved from the living room to the hallway and stopped. She looked at her phone and considered calling the non-emergency line and decided against it and in the morning asked her neighbor if everything was all right, and her neighbor said it was and looked at her in a way that made her not entirely believe it.

She had been working from home for three weeks and had fallen into the habit of leaving the curtains open for the light, which she was now reconsidering. The man on the sidewalk had stopped outside her window twice in the past week — once for long enough to look at his phone, once for long enough to look at the window. She could not see him clearly through the frosted lower portion of the glass and she could not tell if he could see her. She moved her desk three feet to the left, which put her out of the direct sightline from the street. She kept the curtains as they were, because closing them would tell her something she preferred not to know. She mentioned the man to her neighbor in the next flat and her neighbor said she had not noticed anyone, which was not the same as there being no one.

The property had been on the market for six months and she had toured it alone on three occasions and was going to make an offer and had decided in the parking lot beforehand that she was going to check the basement first, which she had not checked on the first two visits because the previous two agents had always taken her upstairs immediately. She found the basement door unlocked and the basement empty and with the particular smell of damp and disuse that was entirely consistent with a property that had been vacant for six months. She noted a second door at the far end of the basement — exterior access, secured from the inside with a bolt that was currently unbolted. She bolted it and stood in the basement and looked at the unfinished ceiling and the bare concrete floor and the single bulb and told herself this was a good property and the bolt could easily have been left undone by the last viewer and drove home and did not make the offer that week.

She kept the radio on in the evenings now, not for the programs but for the presence of it — the sense of another voice in the house. She had been doing this for three weeks, since the incident with the car, and she had not told anyone she was doing it because describing it aloud would require her to decide whether it was a precaution or a symptom and she was not ready to make that determination. She checked the locks before bed in a sequence she had established: front door, back door, kitchen window, living room window, bathroom window. The sequence took two minutes and thirty seconds. She had not timed it intentionally. She was aware that she knew how long it took. She was managing well, she thought, and the radio played in the evenings and the locks were good locks and she slept in a house that was secured against whatever the secured locks secured a house against.

The door to the archives room was supposed to be locked at all times outside of regular hours and it was not locked when she tried it at seven-fifteen on a Tuesday morning, which was before regular hours. She stood in the doorway and turned on the light and looked at the rows of shelving and the long table with its box of white cotton gloves and the climate control unit and found nothing obviously wrong. She went to the shelf she had last been working at and found the box she had left exactly where she had left it and found the documents within it in the order in which she had organized them. She could not have said what she had expected to find and she found everything as it should be. She relocked the door and went to the head archivist's office and left a note about the unlocked door and went to her own office and sat at her desk and spent the first hour of her workday unable to concentrate fully on the task in front of her.

She had interviewed enough difficult subjects to know that the man sitting across from her was managing what he told her with a precision that went beyond normal caution. He answered her questions with answers that were complete and accurate and gave her nothing she had not already asked for, which was the technique of someone with practice. She noted which questions produced the longest pauses before he answered and which produced no pause at all, and the pattern was informative. When she left the interview she sat in her car in his driveway for a moment and made notes while the interview was fresh. She had nothing specific. She had the shape of something she could not yet see directly, and the shape of it was not reassuring.

She was alone in the house for the first time in three months and the house felt larger than it had when Daniel was in it, and louder in the specific way that empty houses were loud — each of its sounds more present without the background of another person's presence to absorb them. She found herself listening to them: the refrigerator cycling, the furnace, the pipes in the upstairs bathroom that had always knocked once in the night. She was not afraid of the house. She had lived alone for eight years before Daniel and she had done it without incident. She checked the front door and the back door out of a thoroughness she preferred to call habit and went to bed and lay in the large quiet of the house and fell asleep listening to the familiar sounds of it, cataloguing each one, reminding herself of its source.

The GPS on her phone showed her route home as she drove it and she had not opened the app but it was open, which meant something had opened it, and she was running through the explanations for this — pocket-opened, auto-opened, something she didn't remember doing — and none of them were satisfying in the way that explanations were supposed to be satisfying. She looked at the app and its blue dot tracking her movement and turned it off and then, after a moment, turned location services off entirely. She drove home by a different route than the one she usually drove and parked in the guest lot rather than her assigned space. In the morning she was going to talk to the person at work who knew about these things. Tonight she double-checked the app permissions on her phone and found three that she did not remember granting and revoked all three and sat on her couch with the phone in her hand feeling the specific unease of a problem she could not yet precisely name.

The boat was tied up where she had left it and looked undisturbed, which was what she had expected and also what she had been hoping, which were not always the same category of expectation. She untied it and checked the engine and the fuel and the safety kit and the flare gun and found everything as she had left it. She checked the storage compartment under the stern seat, which was where she kept the additional equipment, and found it also as she had left it. She had been checking things more carefully since March, since the conversation in the parking lot of the marina, and she was aware of checking more carefully and was at peace with this awareness. She started the engine and idled out of the slip and turned north and the morning was clear and the water was flat and by the time she cleared the harbor mouth she was paying attention to the water rather than to what she had been paying attention to, which was the right order of priorities.

The woman she had seen at the conference was at her grocery store on Saturday, which was unusual but not impossible — conferences drew people from the same city, after all. She was at the hardware store on Monday, which was less easily explained. She appeared on Thursday outside the building where she had a dentist appointment, which was information of a different kind. She had not spoken to the woman and the woman had not approached her and she could not have said, if asked, that anything specific had happened. But the accumulation of it was specific in the way that individual innocuous events became specific when they accumulated, and she described the accumulation to her friend Kate over coffee and Kate said that was a lot and she agreed that it was.

She drove past the motel twice before deciding it was the right level of ordinary — visible from the highway, cash payment accepted, the kind of place where the night clerk did not look up from the television when you signed in. She had been driving for six hours and she needed to stop and she had stopped calling ahead because of what had happened at the last place she had called ahead to. She paid for one night and asked for a room at the back and parked her car around the side where it was not visible from the road and she sat in the room with the chain on the door and the chair under the handle, which she had read about and found less reassuring in practice than in theory. She called no one. She ate the crackers she had bought at a gas station two hours ago. She lay on the bed in her clothes with the lamp on and slept lightly for four hours and was on the road before it was light.

He had offered to walk her to her car after the late meeting, which was a courteous offer, and she had said no because she was parked in the well-lit lot and it was only two hundred feet and it had been a professional context and saying yes would have required her to explain to herself why she was saying yes. She walked to the car at a steady pace and she did not look behind her and she would not have heard footsteps over the sound of the wind and the distant traffic and she was at her car and in it and the doors were locked before any of these considerations became relevant. She sat for a moment with the engine off. She started the engine. She drove home by a route that passed two police stations, which was not a shorter route.

The front window of the shop had been egged on Tuesday night — not vandalism, the officer had said, probably just kids. Probably just kids. She had heard this phrase before as a coda to things that were and were not just kids, and she had thanked the officer and cleaned the window and installed the external camera she had been putting off installing for six months. She reviewed the camera's first week of footage on the following Sunday and found nothing concerning, which was reassuring in the ordinary way that nothing concerning was reassuring, which was to say completely and only for a week at a time. She backed up the footage to the cloud and changed the system's default password to something that would not appear on a list of default passwords, and she put the officer's card in the drawer by the cash register, and she opened the shop on Monday as she opened it every Monday.

She had run the same five-mile route for two years and knew it well enough to run it in the dark on the mornings she started before sunrise. Tonight she was running it at dusk, which was not her usual time, because of the schedule change, and dusk was different from dark in a way she had not thought about before running it at dusk. Dark was committed. Dusk was in between. The path along the river was less populated than the street route and she had always preferred the river and she stayed on the river and she was aware of the way the light was going and of being aware of the way the light was going, which was a category of self-awareness she did not particularly enjoy. She completed the route and stretched on her front steps and went inside and locked the door and noted that she had run the route at dusk and that the next time she would run it at a different time.

The rental car was the same make as hers, which she had not thought about when she chose it, and she had been unsettled for twenty minutes driving it before she understood why — it felt familiar in a way that something unfamiliar should not feel familiar, and familiarity in an unfamiliar context was a category of thing she had learned to pay attention to. She checked the car before she got in it in the morning and before she got in it in the evening: she had started doing this without deciding to start and she did it efficiently and without drama, the way you checked a lock or a pilot light, because the alternative to checking was not-knowing and she had established a preference for knowing. The rental period was four days. She drove it four days and returned it and collected her own car from the long-term lot and sat in it for a moment in the familiar smell of it and felt something ease that she had not noticed was tight.

She had been staying in the cabin for a week and had fallen into a routine with it, the way you fell into routines with the places you stayed alone — the same mug for the morning coffee, the same walk in the afternoon, the same window she checked before bed. The window looked out over the lake and the tree line on the far shore and the small dock where her borrowed kayak was tied. On the fifth night the kayak was at a different angle than she had tied it. She stood at the window for a long time looking at this fact. A boat passing in the night could have moved it. The wind, which had been up that day, could have moved it. She went down to the dock in the morning and checked the kayak and found it undamaged and retied it the way she always tied it and went back up to the cabin and checked the door lock, which was a simple lock, and noted that she would sleep better with a better lock.

The first shot took out the window above her head and she hit the floor before she had consciously decided to move. Glass rained down across her back and shoulders. She could hear him reloading somewhere to her left — a small, mechanical sound that was the most frightening thing she had ever heard. She pressed herself flat against the hardwood and tried to think. The door was twelve feet away. The door was twelve feet of open floor with no cover. She looked at the window and calculated the drop and decided the window was worse. Another shot, this one into the wall two feet from her head, plaster dust drifting down onto her hair. She was aware that she was shaking and filed this information away as irrelevant. She gathered herself and ran for the door in a low scramble, felt something burn across her upper arm, hit the door with her shoulder, and was through it and into the hallway, her legs still moving before she understood she had made it.

He had her pinned to the floor with his knee in her back and her arm wrenched up behind her at an angle that sent white light across her vision. She made herself breathe. Panicking would not help. She was aware of the cold of the concrete against her cheek and the smell of mildew and motor oil and the sound of his breathing, controlled and patient. He told her to stop moving. She stopped moving and catalogued her options, which were few and poor. Her right hand was free. She moved it slowly toward his ankle, feeling for the knife she had seen him put there. He noticed and wrenched her arm higher and she heard something in her shoulder make a sound that scared her. She went still again. She was going to have to wait. Waiting was something she could do. She closed her eyes and slowed her breathing and waited.

The fire had started in the equipment room and was moving faster than any fire she had ever seen, which was saying something because she had seen fires. She got two of the ranch hands out through the side door before the roof of the corridor came down. The heat was a physical force now, something that pressed against her face and hands with intent. She could not see more than eight feet in any direction through the smoke. Someone was shouting her name from outside and she followed the sound, keeping low, one hand on the wall. The wall was hot. She pulled her hand back and kept moving. She found the third ranch hand unconscious against the back wall of the barn, already beyond the fire line, and she grabbed his collar and pulled. He was two hundred and twenty pounds and she was one hundred and thirty-five and it did not matter. She got him to the door. She got him through it. She dropped to her knees on the gravel outside and breathed.

The truck hit her car on the driver's side and the world rotated ninety degrees and kept going. She was aware of the airbag and then she was aware of nothing and then she was aware of being upside down with the engine still running and something wet on her face. She got her seatbelt off and fell onto what had been the ceiling, the impact jarring through her already-aching shoulders. Her right knee had hit something on the way around and was singing a high, clear note of pain that she could not afford to pay attention to. She found the window and it was gone — blown out in the roll — and she pulled herself through it and onto the asphalt of the highway, the rough surface scraping her palms open. She lay there for exactly two seconds, which was all she allowed herself. The truck had stopped twenty yards away. She could see the driver's door opening and a large man stepping out. She looked at the ditch on the far side of the road and made the calculation. She started moving before he had fully cleared the vehicle, keeping low, keeping the wrecked car between them, and she did not look back.

He had her by the throat with both hands and her feet were off the floor. She clawed at his wrists and got nothing. Black was beginning to come in at the edges of her vision. She made herself stop clawing — it was doing nothing and costing her oxygen she did not have. She brought her knee up hard and missed, caught his thigh instead. He tightened his grip and she felt something in her throat make a clicking sound that terrified her. Her hands dropped to her sides. She found the pen in her jacket pocket by feel, a heavy metal pen, and drove it into the inside of his elbow with everything she had left. His grip loosened a fraction — enough. She dropped her weight suddenly, broke his hold, and hit the floor on all fours, dragging air in through a throat that felt like gravel. She stayed on all fours for three seconds, which was all she could afford, and then she got up.

The rope had been on her wrists for four hours and her hands had stopped being numb and started being nothing at all. She was working on the knot by feel in the dark of the cellar, her fingers clumsy and remote, and she was making progress — slow progress, but progress. She could hear footsteps above her, two sets, moving back and forth. She had been counting the intervals between check-ins: twenty-two minutes, seventeen minutes, nineteen minutes. She would call it eighteen minutes. She needed four of those minutes to get the knot loose, based on current rate. She had gotten the outer loop free. She could feel the next wrap. The footsteps stopped. She went still and counted her own heartbeats and at thirty-one the footsteps started again, moving away from the cellar door. She went back to the knot. Her hands had started to tingle and she took this as good news.

They came out of the trees on the south side of the property and she counted three of them before she stopped counting and grabbed Cole's arm and pulled him down behind the woodpile. She could hear them talking, low and unhurried, as if they had all the time they needed, which they probably did. Her phone had no signal — she had checked it twice in the last hour and was done checking. Cole's rifle was inside the house, which was also where they could not go without crossing open ground. She looked at the distance from the woodpile to the back door of the house and thought about it carefully and decided it was not survivable. One of them had posted himself at the corner of the barn with a clear sightline to the back door. She looked at the gap between the barn and the fence line and thought about that instead — a gap of perhaps four feet, in shadow, angled away from the man at the corner. She told Cole what she was going to do and he told her she was not going to do it and she looked at him in the way that ended that conversation. She handed him her phone to try for signal and went.

The explosion threw her across the room and she hit the far wall shoulder-first and slid down it to the floor, the impact rattling through her teeth. Her ears were producing a high tone that was not a sound so much as an absence of sound, a cotton silence that muffled everything. She sat on the floor and tried to think clearly and assessed herself: breathing, yes; vision, cloudy but functioning; the shoulder hurt badly but moved when she told it to. The room was filling with something — smoke, gas, she could not tell which and it did not change the response — and she knew she had perhaps two minutes before it stopped mattering. She found the window by the light coming through it and got to her feet and broke it with her elbow, which hurt exactly as much as she expected it to and she had expected it to hurt a great deal. The air outside was cold and immediate and she breathed it in deliberately. She got one leg through the broken frame and then the other, navigating the remaining glass with care she could not entirely afford. She dropped eight feet into the alley below and took the landing in her legs and rolled the way she had been taught and came up running, her shoulder on fire, her ears still ringing, and she considered this one of her better performances under the circumstances.

He had been stabbed before — once, years ago, a different life — and he recognized the sensation: a cold pressure rather than pain, at first, with the pain arriving approximately three seconds later like an invoice he had been expecting. He pressed his hand against his side and felt the blood, warm and too plentiful, and tried to do the math about how much time that amount of blood represented. The man who had done it was still in the room, watching him with a professional patience that he found, in a distant way, admirable. He was between her and the door and that was the only fact that mattered. He took a step toward the door and the man moved to block it and he took this as information: they needed him mobile, which meant the wound was not immediately fatal, which was the best news available. He straightened up from the involuntary curl his body had attempted and found the pain and acknowledged it and set it aside. He looked the man in the eye and moved toward him with everything he had remaining, which was less than his usual and more than the man had accounted for.

The man had a gun and she had a letter opener and she was under no illusions about the disparity between these two things. She had been thinking about how to close that disparity for six minutes while she worked her way across the carpet toward him without making a sound. She was also behind him, which was the only advantage she had and she intended to use it completely and without hesitation. She crossed the twelve feet between them in absolute silence — the carpet helped, the concentration helped — and brought the heavy weighted base of the letter opener down on the back of his skull with everything she had, putting her shoulder and her hip and her whole body into the motion the way she had been taught. He went down without making a sound, the gun skidding away across the floor, out of his hand and out of her reach. She stood over him and watched him and he stayed down. She crossed to the gun and picked it up and checked it in the way she had been shown — magazine, chamber, safety — and stood at the door with it held in both hands. She had not fired a gun in three years. Her hands were shaking finely, which she acknowledged and set aside. She opened the door.

The man had a gun and she had a letter opener and she was under no illusions about this disparity, which she had been turning over for the past six minutes while she worked her way across the office toward him. She was also behind him, which was the only advantage she had and she intended to use it completely. She crossed the twelve feet between them in absolute silence, which was the skill she was most proud of in that particular moment, the carpet absorbing her footsteps. She brought the heavy base of the letter opener down on the back of his skull with everything she had, putting her whole body into the swing. He went down without a sound, the gun skidding across the floor away from both of them. She stood over him breathing hard, the letter opener still in her hand, watching him to make sure he stayed down. He stayed down. She crossed to the gun and picked it up and checked it the way she had been shown and stood at the door with it in both hands. She had not fired one in three years and her hands were shaking finely and she gripped it the way she had been taught and went to the door and opened it.

The horse threw her at the third fence and she landed badly — she knew it was bad before she stopped moving, the impact resonating up through her hip and into her spine in a way that suggested something had given way. She lay in the paddock on her back and looked at the sky for one second, which was all she permitted. She did the inventory: legs moved, both of them, which was what she needed to know first; hands moved; vision clear and unblurred; breathing hurt on the left side, which was new and indicated at minimum a cracked rib. The horse was circling the paddock, spooked, still running, his eyes white-rimmed and his hooves loud on the hard ground. She needed to get to the rail before he came around again or she needed to get to her feet, and she was going to try for the rail because it was closer. She rolled onto her side and the pain in her left ribs made a strong argument against further movement and she overruled it and got to her knees, one hand on the ground. She got to her feet. She made it to the rail in time to press herself against it as twelve hundred pounds of frightened horse went past two feet away, the wind of his passage moving her hair. She gripped the rail and breathed.

She found him at the bottom of the ravine, which meant he had fallen thirty feet or been pushed. She was not ruling out the second. He was conscious, which was the best thing she saw, and his left leg was wrong in a way that she did not let herself look at directly. She climbed down to him, which took longer than she wanted because the slope was loose shale and hurrying would land her beside him. She got to him and put her hand on his face and he opened his eyes and said her name and she said she was there. She looked at the leg. She looked away. She took off her jacket and her flannel shirt and put her jacket back on and used the flannel shirt in the way she had been taught in a wilderness first aid course she had taken fifteen years ago and had never expected to use. She told him it was going to be fine. She had no basis for this statement. She made it with complete conviction.

She had dislocated her shoulder getting the cuffs off the pipe and now it hung at a wrong angle that she was resolutely not looking at directly because looking at it would use attention she needed elsewhere. She had popped a shoulder back in once before — college, a climbing accident — and she remembered the mechanics of how it was done. She was not going to do it here in this room before she knew what was on the other side of the door, because she would need both hands for what came after and the process of relocating a shoulder was not a quiet one. She tucked the arm against her body, which helped with the pain to a degree she would call manageable, and went to the door. It was locked, which she had expected, and it was a standard interior lock, a push-button privacy lock, which she had hoped for because it was the most optimistic type of lock to be behind. She had kept a hairpin in her jacket pocket for exactly this category of situation ever since a self-defense instructor had taught her the technique five years ago. She worked the lock in the dark by feel and tried to do it quietly and managed both. The door opened inward. The hallway beyond was dim, lit only by a window at the far end, and as far as she could see it was empty. She went into it with her good shoulder against the wall.

The second man came through the window while she was still dealing with the first and she understood in that moment that she was not going to get through this cleanly. She drove her elbow into the first man's solar plexus and felt him fold and turned to the second man and took his fist on her cheekbone, which was worse than she had prepared for. Her vision went briefly abstract. She hit the wall and used it to push off and went low and took him at the knees and brought him down and was on top of him before he had finished falling. The first man was getting up. She could hear him. She dealt with the man beneath her with focused efficiency and stood and turned to the first man, her cheekbone burning, her hands steady. He looked at her. He made a decision. He went back out the window.

She shot him in the shoulder because she had aimed for the shoulder, a decision she had made before she raised the gun, which she wanted him to understand. He went down and stayed down and she crossed the room to him in four steps and took the gun from his hand and the knife from his belt with a practiced efficiency that surprised even her. She stood over him and told him he was going to give her the name and he was going to do it in the next sixty seconds because she had places to be. He told her she didn't have it in her to do anything worse. She reminded him, in a conversational tone, that she had just shot him in the shoulder when she could have shot him in the knee, which was a more instructive location. She reminded him that she still had his gun. She reminded him that no one knew she was in this building. He looked up at her from the floor with the recalibrated expression of a man who has made a significant error in his initial assessment of a situation. He looked at the gun. He looked at her face. He gave her the name.

The knife went into his side between the third and fourth ribs and he stood there for a moment looking down at it with an expression of genuine surprise, as though his body had done something impolite. Then his legs made their decision and she was already moving, crossing the distance between them before he had finished falling. She caught him — she was not close enough to catch him properly but she caught him anyway, going down with him to the asphalt, her knees taking the impact. Her hands moved immediately to the wound, applying the pressure she had been taught and had hoped never to use. He was trying to say something and she told him firmly to be quiet and to breathe and that she was there and none of it was going anywhere. She was looking around the alley for something to use and there was nothing useful and she pulled off her scarf one-handed, awkwardly, and pressed it against the wound and leaned her weight into it with both palms flat. She told him again to breathe and counted his breaths and kept her weight on the wound and did not look at the blood on the ground, which was a quantity she could not afford to think about. She kept counting his breaths. He kept breathing.

The building was coming down floor by floor from the top and she had two floors to go and the staircase on the north side was already gone. She had found this out the expensive way — three steps onto the north staircase before the vibration in the risers told her what the smoke had not — and she had backed off it immediately and gone to the south staircase and found it intact and was now moving down it faster than was entirely safe, which was nonetheless the right speed. The floor above her came down with a sound like the end of an argument and the south staircase shuddered under her feet and kept holding and she kept moving, one hand on the rail, her eyes on the steps. One floor remaining between her and the lobby. She could see through the smoke below her the grey rectangle of open doors, the street beyond them containing people who were running away from the building rather than toward it, which told her everything she needed to know about the situation outside. The staircase shuddered again, a longer tremor this time, and she stopped calculating and started running, hit the lobby floor at speed, and went through the open doors into the cold air of the street without slowing.

He was drowning and she knew it and the boat was fifteen feet away and fifteen feet of dark November water was a categorically different distance from fifteen feet of anything else. She went in anyway, because the alternative was watching him drown, and she had decided some time ago that there were things she would not watch. The cold hit her like a physical blow — immediate and total and breathtaking in the literal sense. She found him by the sound he was making, which was not language anymore, and got her arm across his chest from behind the way she had been taught and felt him grab at her with the panic strength of someone who is genuinely drowning. She talked to him — low, steady, her mouth at his ear, continuous — while she worked to move them both toward the boat, because the voice was the only thing that reached past the panic. He was fighting her. She did not take it personally. The boat was ten feet away. Eight. She got to the ladder on the stern and got his hands on the lowest rung and told him to climb in a voice that left no room for any other option. He climbed. She came up after him and they collapsed on the deck and she lay on her back and looked at the sky and started counting his breaths and told him to keep breathing until he told her to stop telling him.

She was on the third-floor ledge with eight inches of stone between her feet and a forty-foot drop and the window behind her had locked automatically when it swung closed, which she had not anticipated and would remember as a lesson for the future, assuming a future. She pressed her back against the glass and made herself look outward rather than down, which was the correct direction to look. She assessed what she had: the ledge, eight inches; the window to her left, six feet away, closed but possibly unlocked; the drainpipe four feet to her right, cast iron, running from the roof gutter to the ground, bolted to the wall at three-foot intervals. She chose the drainpipe because it was closer and because the window was six feet away and six feet on eight inches of ledge was a problem she was not confident of solving. She moved to the right in small sideways steps with her back flat to the building and her palms on the stone. Four feet became three, became two. She reached the drainpipe and gripped it with both hands and tested her weight against it and felt it hold. She went down it hand over hand with her feet braced against the wall and dropped the last four feet to the ground and stood up and put her hand on her knee and breathed for a moment before she started walking.

The avalanche came down the north face of the ridge and she had perhaps four seconds of warning — a sound like a freight train accelerating, a trembling in the ground that moved up through the soles of her boots — before the wall of snow hit. She threw herself behind the largest boulder she could reach in two running steps and put her back to it and made herself as small as possible and the snow came over her like something with a specific intention. She held her position against the boulder with everything she had. The weight was extraordinary — not the light powder of recreational skiing but something dense and cold and massive, pressing against her from every direction simultaneously. When the movement stopped she was in the dark and she could not immediately move her legs, which she took as information rather than cause for alarm. She worked her arms first, pushing space away from her face and chest. Then she worked her legs, which took four minutes that she counted aloud to stay present in herself. She found the direction of the surface by the relative cold of the air on her face and dug toward it with her hands and came out of the snowpack into grey afternoon light and sat on top of the debris field and breathed deliberately and slowly for a long time before she tried to stand.

She shot him in the shoulder because she had aimed for the shoulder, a decision she had made clearly and in advance, which she wanted him to understand. He went down and stayed down and she went to him without hesitation and took the gun from his hand and the knife from his belt, checking both efficiently. She stood over him and told him he was going to give her the name and he was going to do it in the next sixty seconds, because she had made this decision as well. He told her she didn't have it in her to do anything worse than what she'd already done. She looked at him for a moment with an expression she kept carefully neutral. She reminded him that she had shot him in the shoulder when she could have shot him somewhere that would have ended the conversation permanently. She pointed out that she was still holding both his weapons. She noted that no one knew she was in this building and that she had arrived here with a specific purpose that had not yet been accomplished. He looked up at her from the floor with the recalibrated attention of a man revising an important estimate. He gave her the name.

The current had her and she was not going to fight it the way people always tried to fight rivers, which was the way people drowned in rivers. She let it take her and kept her feet up and her face up and kept her eyes moving, looking for the bend in the river where she knew the current would slow. She hit a rock with her hip and spun and lost her cardinal directions and found them again using the sky. She was cold in the specific way that is past feeling — past the sharp stage and into the absence stage — which she catalogued as dangerous and filed under problems for later. The cold had moved up past her knees. She thought about warmth with deliberate and focused intention: the kitchen in her house, the specific weight of the wool blanket on the couch, the temperature of the water in her shower set exactly where she liked it. She used this thinking as an anchor. The bend in the river was visible now and she began to angle toward the bank using the current's own energy rather than opposing it, making small adjustments that cost less than they earned. She felt the water slow around her legs and got her feet down onto gravel and stood up in three feet of water and walked to the bank and sat down in the mud and began to shake, which was exactly right. Shaking meant her body was still working on her behalf.

He came at her with the crowbar and she stepped inside the arc of the swing rather than away from it, which was counterintuitive and which she had practiced enough times that it was also instinct. The crowbar caught her left arm instead of her skull and she felt the impact as a deep concussive force that traveled all the way to her back teeth, and her arm went offline for approximately three seconds. She drove the heel of her right hand into his nose while his momentum was still carrying him forward, putting her weight behind it, and heard something give in a way that told her the damage was real. She saw his eyes go wet and unfocused and used those seconds to drive her knee up into his midsection, which brought him forward and down. He went to the floor. Her left arm was coming back online now in the form of a deep pain that was going to be significantly worse in an hour and she acknowledged this as a future problem. She looked at the arm: wrong, but not compound wrong, not bone-through-skin wrong. She moved it deliberately from elbow to wrist until it did approximately what she asked of it. She looked at him on the floor. She stepped over him and went to the door.

The man threw her into the back of the van and the doors slammed closed and she was in the dark, the cold metal floor under her palms, the van already in motion before she had processed the sequence of events that had led to this. She gave herself five seconds to be frightened and then she stopped. She had twelve seconds before she lost track of direction and she used them carefully: left turn immediately, then a hard right, then acceleration — highway on-ramp, she thought, which meant they were heading either north or south out of the city. She explored the cargo area by feel, moving efficiently: wheel well on the right side, a locked toolbox, a coil of rope she moved away from herself, and — mounted above the rear wheel well — a fire extinguisher. She took the fire extinguisher off its bracket and held it and thought about the van's rear doors, which were double-panel and hinged on the outside. She thought about the door handles, also on the outside. She thought about the hinges, which were on the inside. The van was traveling at highway speed. She braced her back against the wheel well and began to work on the hinge pins with the bracket end of the extinguisher, methodically, one at a time, in the dark.

The second bullet was closer than the first and she understood from the angle and the grouping that he was walking his shots in toward her position, which meant she needed to move before he completed the walk. She had been behind the overturned conference table for forty-three seconds and it had been adequate cover and it was about to stop being adequate cover. She looked at the distance to the kitchen doorway — fifteen feet of open floor, no intermediate cover, bad odds — and then at the window to her right, which was closer but opened onto the wrong side of the building. She thought about the angle of the shots and calculated his probable position and decided the doorway was survivable if she went low and fast and did not pause to reconsider at any point during the crossing, because reconsideration was the thing that got people killed in doorway situations. She checked what she had in her hands, which was a phone with no signal and a stapler, and put the stapler in her jacket pocket because weight in the hand was better than nothing. She gathered herself behind the table. She identified the line she was going to run. She went on the count of two, not three, because three was what people expected.

She had been tied to the chair for six hours and her shoulders had moved from burning to a deep, structural ache that suggested the damage was real. She had been working on the knot at her left wrist by pressing her thumbnail into the rope and working each strand separately, which was slow and was the only option available. The room was a storage room of some kind — shelving, cardboard boxes, a bare bulb overhead that had been left on. She could hear two voices through the wall and had been tracking their conversation for the quality of information it offered, which was limited. She had learned there were two of them, that they were waiting for a call, and that one of them wanted to leave before the call came. The disagreement about this had been ongoing for forty minutes. She pressed her thumbnail into the next strand of rope. She had the outer wrap free now and was working on the second layer. The disagreement through the wall was escalating. She worked faster.

The gunshot wound in her leg had gone through cleanly, which she had been told twice was good news, and she was trying to believe this while she tied her belt above the wound and pulled it tight and felt the bleeding slow to something less catastrophic. She was in the lee of a boulder on an open hillside with two men between her and the tree line and she had used her last round getting herself this far. She had a belt, a pocket knife, her phone with no signal, and the knowledge that the tree line was sixty yards away across open ground. She looked at the light, which was going. She looked at the ground between her and the trees. She identified the shallow drainage ditch that ran at an angle from the boulder toward the tree line and offered approximately eighteen inches of cover if she stayed flat. Sixty yards in eighteen inches of cover on a leg that was going to object strenuously. She pulled the belt tighter. She got onto her stomach. She started moving.

The man hit the water the same time she did and she understood immediately that he was a stronger swimmer, which changed her calculation. She dove deep and went for the bottom, where the visibility was zero and the advantage shifted. She could feel the pressure change as he followed her down. She changed direction twice using the current to mask the movement. Her lungs were beginning to register their opinion of this situation. She found the piling she had been aiming for and got behind it and waited, her hand on the barnacled wood, looking up toward the lesser dark where the surface was. He passed above her, his body a shadow against the faint light. She waited until he had gone three feet past and then she surfaced behind him and reached for him, and the water around them churned, and she was stronger than he had assessed her to be, which was the last mistake he made.

The tree came down across the road in front of them and he hit the brakes and the truck slid on the wet asphalt and stopped with the bumper six inches from the trunk. She had the door open before the truck had fully stopped. The tree was too large to move and too solid to drive over and the rain was coming down in the way that eliminated visibility beyond fifteen feet. She heard the other engine before she saw the lights — coming up fast behind them, not slowing. She grabbed his arm and they went over the guard rail on the passenger side together, dropping eight feet into the brush below the road, landing badly in the wet undergrowth. The other vehicle hit the tree at speed and the sound of it was enormous and then there was silence except for the rain. She lay in the brush and listened and looked up at the road above them. A door opened. Footsteps on the asphalt. She put her hand on his arm and felt him go still beside her.

She woke on the floor of an unfamiliar room with her hands bound in front of her and a headache that told her something had struck her skull. She lay still and did not open her eyes and listened. One person in the room, moving quietly, not looking at her — she could tell by the sound of the footsteps that they were directed away. She opened her eyes to a careful slit and confirmed: one man, near the window, looking out at something. She assessed her bound hands: zip ties, not rope, which was worse in some ways and better in others. She was on carpet. She was wearing her own clothes. Her shoes were still on, which mattered. She found the corner of the room with her eyes and found the door, which was closed and on the far side of the man at the window. She found what was between her and the door. She began to plan the route.

The fire had gotten into the roof structure and she could hear it moving overhead like something with intention and appetite. She had the two children from the back bedroom and she needed to get them down the stairs before the ceiling made that decision for her. The older one could move on her own. The younger one could not. She picked up the younger one and told the older one to hold onto her jacket and not let go regardless of what happened, in a voice that communicated the non-negotiable nature of this instruction. She went to the door and felt it with her palm — hot, but not the kind of hot that meant fire immediately on the other side — and opened it. The hallway was smoke but passable if they stayed low.
